name: Go
description: The oldest continuously played board game, originating in China over 2,500 years ago, with a game-tree complexity of 10^360 that dwarfs chess's 10^123 by more than the number of atoms in the observable universe. South Korea admits only four new professionals per year from a pool of 80 candidates, most of whom began full-time training before age 12.

thumbnail.alt: Black and white stones on a wooden Go board during a professional match

media.title: AlphaGo - The Movie | Full award-winning documentary (DeepMind)

sources.title: Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks and tree search
sources.author: David Silver, Aja Huang, Chris J. Maddison et al.

time_to_competence.notes: Rules are minimal (can be learned in minutes), but playing a coherent game requires understanding of life/death, territory, and basic joseki, typically weeks to months.

time_to_expert.notes: Professional players typically begin training as children and study 8-12 hours daily for 5-10+ years. The median age for achieving professional 1-dan is mid-to-late teens after a decade of focused training.

feedback_delay.notes: Each game ends with a clear score, but the consequences of strategic decisions made in the opening (fuseki) may not be apparent for 50-100 moves. AI review now provides instant post-game analysis.

failure_cost.notes: A lost game costs only rating points. However, failing professional examinations in Korea or Japan can end a career path entirely. Aspiring pros in Japan's insei system face age-based expulsion.

cognitive_demand.notes: Branching factor of ~250 per move (vs chess's ~35). Players must simultaneously evaluate territory, influence, life/death status of groups, and ko threats across a 361-point board.

physical_demand.notes: Seated activity. Professional title matches can span multiple days, but the exertion is purely mental.

prerequisite_knowledge.notes: Rules are self-contained and simpler than chess. No mathematical or domain-specific prerequisites. Strategic concepts emerge from play.

measurability.notes: Kyu/dan ranking system plus Elo-based online ratings (KGS, OGS, Fox) provide precise measurement. Professional ranks (1p-9p) are formally certified by national organizations.

perishability.notes: Positional intuition and reading ability are durable, but tactical sharpness and familiarity with current AI-influenced joseki decay with inactivity. Opening theory now evolves rapidly post-AlphaGo.

precision_required.notes: At the professional level, a single suboptimal move can shift territorial balance irreversibly. The margin between winning and losing is often 0.5-2 points in a ~300-point game.

ambiguity.notes: Games end with a precise numerical score, but positional evaluation during play involves assessing nebulous concepts like 'thickness' and 'influence' that resist quantification even for AI.

instruction_dependency.notes: Self-study is viable via books and AI tools, but the East Asian professional path relies heavily on mentorship. In Japan, each professional belongs to a master's study group.

environmental_variability.notes: Identical starting position (empty board) every game. No randomness whatsoever. Black plays first with komi compensation for White.

concurrent_skills.notes: Simultaneously managing whole-board strategy, local tactical reading, time management, group status evaluation, and endgame counting. Ko fights add a meta-strategic layer unique to Go.

plateau_density.notes: Notorious for long plateaus, especially at the dan boundary (1-dan is called 'the wall'). Progress from mid-dan to professional level can take years of apparently static improvement.

domain_evolution.notes: AlphaGo's 2016 victory triggered the most significant upheaval in Go theory in centuries. Many traditional joseki were overturned. Professional opening theory now evolves rapidly.

observability.notes: To an uninitiated observer, a Go board looks like random stones. The strategic depth is completely invisible without expert commentary.

luck_variance.notes: No random elements. The only variance comes from nigiri (random selection of who plays Black), compensated by komi.

social_coordination.notes: Purely a two-player contest. Study groups and teacher-student relationships exist but the competition itself is solo.

methodology_notes: Scores grounded in Wikipedia's comprehensive Go articles, the Silver et al. Nature paper on AlphaGo's game-tree complexity analysis, and documented professional training systems in Japan (insei), Korea, and China. The game-tree complexity figure (10^360) and branching factor (~250) come from peer-reviewed computational research. Training timelines derived from documented career histories of professional players.
